fidelityNote: Per the NCEES FE Reference Handbook v10.6 (Civil Eng. → Groundwater, p. 297–299 and Environmental Eng. → Remediation, p. 330): Darcy's Law $Q=-KA\,dh/dx$, the specific discharge $q=-K\,dh/dx$, seepage velocity $v=q/n$, the constant-head test $K=Q/(iAt_e)$, the falling-head test $K=2.303\,(aL)/(At_e)\log_{10}(h_1/h_2)$, transmissivity $T=Kb$, Dupuit's Formula (unconfined well), and the Thiem Equation (confined well) are all printed in v10.6. The Retardation Factor $R=1+(\rho_b/n_e)K_d$, $K_d=K_{oc}f_{oc}$, and Vadose Zone Penetration $D=R_vV/A$ are in the Environmental Engineering section of v10.6. Soil index properties (void ratio, porosity, etc.) appear in the Civil Engineering — Geotechnical section. The contaminant velocity relation $v_c=v/R$ is a direct consequence of the Retardation Factor definition and is flagged as fundamental.

questions.stem: A soil sample has a total volume of 100 cm³ and a void volume of 35 cm³. The porosity n of the soil is:
A sandy soil has a void ratio e = 0.65. The porosity n is most nearly:
An aquifer that is bounded above by an impermeable confining layer (aquitard) so that the groundwater is under pressure greater than atmospheric is called a:
questions.solution: A confined (artesian) aquifer is overlain by an impermeable confining layer; water pressure exceeds atmospheric, so water in a tightly cased well rises above the top of the aquifer. An unconfined (water-table) aquifer has a free surface exposed to atmospheric pressure.

questions.stem: A saturated sandy formation has a hydraulic conductivity K = 4.0 × 10⁻⁴ m/s. The hydraulic gradient across a flow cross-section of A = 2.0 m² is i = 0.015. The Darcy discharge rate Q is most nearly:

Two monitoring wells are 120 m apart along the direction of groundwater flow. Water-level elevations are 23.5 m and 21.8 m above the same datum (flow is from the high well to the low well). The hydraulic gradient i is most nearly:
An aquifer has hydraulic conductivity K = 3.0 × 10⁻⁵ m/s, hydraulic gradient i = 0.010, and effective porosity n = 0.25. The average seepage (pore-water) velocity v is most nearly:
questions.solution: $q = Ki = (3.0\times10^{-5})(0.010) = 3.0\times10^{-7}\ \mathrm{m/s}$.  $v = q/n = 3.0\times10^{-7}/0.25 = 1.2\times10^{-6}\ \mathrm{m/s}$. The seepage velocity is larger than the Darcy velocity because water flows only through the pore space (fraction n of the total area).

questions.stem: A constant-head permeability test on a sand sample yields: total discharge Q = 1.5 × 10⁻⁵ m³, elapsed time tₑ = 60 s, hydraulic gradient i = 0.80, specimen cross-sectional area A = 5 × 10⁻³ m². The hydraulic conductivity K is most nearly:
A confined aquifer has a hydraulic conductivity K = 1.2 × 10⁻⁴ m/s and a saturated thickness b = 25 m. The transmissivity T is most nearly:

A confined aquifer has a hydraulic conductivity K = 5 × 10⁻⁵ ft/s. Two monitoring wells spaced 600 ft apart show a head drop of 4.8 ft. The flow cross-sectional area is 1,200 ft². Find the Darcy discharge Q (in ft³/s).

An unconfined aquifer well has reached steady-state pumping. Given K = 1.5 × 10⁻⁴ ft/s, h₁ = 22 ft (saturated height at well, r₁ = 2 ft), h₂ = 32 ft (at r₂ = 400 ft), the steady pumping rate Q is most nearly:
A confined aquifer (b = 30 m, K = 8 × 10⁻⁵ m/s) is pumped to steady state. Piezometric heads: h₁ = 18 m at r₁ = 0.2 m and h₂ = 25 m at r₂ = 200 m. The pumping rate Q is most nearly:
A municipal well is pumped at a rate Q = 0.050 cfs and the resulting drawdown at the well is Dw = 8.0 ft. The specific capacity of the well is:

A falling-head permeability test on a clay sample gives: standpipe area a = 1 cm², specimen cross-sectional area A = 50 cm², specimen length L = 15 cm, elapsed time tₑ = 480 s, initial head h₁ = 80 cm, final head h₂ = 40 cm. The hydraulic conductivity K is most nearly:

questions.concept: Dupuit's Formula — pumping rate in gpm (USCS)
questions.stem: An unconfined aquifer well reaches steady state with the following data: K = 2.0 × 10⁻⁴ ft/s, well radius r₁ = 1.5 ft with saturated height h₁ = 30 ft, observation well at r₂ = 600 ft with h₂ = 45 ft. Find the steady-state pumping rate Q in gallons per minute (1 ft³/s = 448.8 gpm).

A confined aquifer has transmissivity T = 3.5 × 10⁻³ m²/s. At steady state, piezometric heads are h₁ = 12 m at r₁ = 0.25 m (well face) and h₂ = 18 m at r₂ = 250 m. Determine the pumping rate Q (in m³/s).
A contaminant in an aquifer has a retardation factor R = 4.0. If the average seepage velocity of the groundwater is 0.50 m/day, the velocity of the contaminant plume is most nearly:
questions.solution: $v_c = v/R = 0.50/4.0 = 0.125\ \mathrm{m/day}$. The retardation factor R expresses how much slower a sorbing contaminant travels relative to the average pore-water velocity. R = 1 means no retardation (conservative tracer).

questions.concept: Distribution coefficient Kd from Koc and foc
questions.stem: A soil has a fraction of organic carbon foc = 0.02 and an organic carbon partition coefficient Koc = 150 mL/g. The distribution coefficient Kd is most nearly:
questions.handbook: Remediation — Soil-Water Partition Coefficient / Retardation Factor

questions.stem: A 0.5 m³ gasoline spill occurs over a surface area of 4 m² at a site with medium to fine sand (Rv = 80). Using the Handbook vadose zone penetration formula, the maximum depth of penetration D is most nearly:

An aquifer has a bulk density ρb = 1.8 g/cm³, effective porosity ne = 0.30, organic carbon partition coefficient Koc = 200 cm³/g, and soil organic carbon fraction foc = 0.01. Compute the retardation factor R.
questions.solution: $K_d = K_{oc}\,f_{oc} = 200\times0.01 = 2.0\ \mathrm{cm^3/g}$.  $R = 1 + \dfrac{\rho_b}{n_e}K_d = 1 + \dfrac{1.8}{0.30}\times2.0 = 1 + 6.0\times2.0 = 1 + 12 = 13$. This means the contaminant travels at 1/13 the groundwater seepage velocity.

questions.stem: In the groundwater seepage velocity equation v = q/n, the value n is the *effective* porosity rather than total porosity because it:
options: includes all pore space, even that occupied by bound or trapped water
excludes pore space occupied by immobile (bound/trapped) water that does not transmit flow
is always larger than total porosity in fine-grained soils
equals the void ratio e for all soil types
questions.solution: Effective porosity is the fraction of pore space through which water actually flows — it excludes isolated pores, dead-end pores, and water held by surface tension or adsorption. Because effective porosity < total porosity, the seepage velocity v = q/n is *higher* than the Darcy velocity q; it better represents the true speed at which dissolved contaminants (non-retarded) travel.
